coredata.dc:title: Construction of fluorescent sensor array with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensing Sudan Orange G and identification of various azo compounds
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Bu, Lutong
Li, Shuangying
Nie, Linchun
Jiang, Liushan
Dong, Guangyu
Song, Denghao
Liu, Wenjing
Geng, Xiaodie
Meng, Dejing
Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were facilely fabricated by one-pot hydrothermal method with levulinic acid and triethanolamine. A fluorescent sensor array was established for identifying azo compounds including Sudan Orange G (SOG), p-diaminoazobenzene, p-aminoazobenzene, azobenzene and quantitative detection of SOG. Experimental results revealed that azo compounds could quench the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs. Owing to various azo compounds showing different affinities to N-CDs, the sensor array exhibited different fluorescence quenching changes, which were further analyzed with principal component analysis to discriminate azo compounds. The sensor array was able to differentiate and recognize diverse concentrations of azo compounds from 0.25 to 2 mg/L. Simultaneously, a variety of factors affecting the detection of SOG were optimized. Under the optimized conditions, the sensor showed excellent stability and sensitivity. The sensor possessed marvelous linearity in the range of 0.1–1 mg/L and 1–4 mg/L and the detection limit was 27.82 μg/L. Spiked recoveries of 90.8–98.2 % were attained at spiked levels of 0.2 mg/L and 1 mg/L, demonstrating that the constructed fluorescence sensor was dependable and feasible for sensing SOG in environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Azo compounds
Fluorescent sensor array
Sudan Orange G
Fluorescence quenching

Characterization of nitrogen doped carbon dots
Influence of the dosage of N-CDs
Influence of incubation time on fluorescence quenching of N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of N-CDs and detection mechanism of prepared sensor array for quantification and identification of azo compounds.

(A) TEM images, size distribution and lattice measurement of N-CDs; (B) Fluorescence spectra at varied excitation wavelengths; (C) 3D contour of prepared N-CDs; (D) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs; (E) XPS spectra of N-CDs.
Optimization of parameters. (A) N-CDs concentration; (B) pH; (C) Incubation time with addition of SOG; (D) NaCl concentration on fluorescence quenching of N-CDs with presence of SOG.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, SOG and their mixtures; (B) The fluorescent emission spectrum of N-CDs and UV–vis absorption of SOG; (C) Fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and solution of N-CDs + SOG.
Linear relationship between F0-F and SOG over ranges of 0.1–1 mg/L (A) and 1–4 mg/L (B); (C) Stability of the constructed sensor.
(A) Fluorescence quenching values of SOG and other azo compounds under same condition. (B) Fluorescence quenching values of SOG and other metal ions under the same condition.
(A) Heat map for four kinds of azo compound pattern recognition; 2D canonical score plots of array sensing system for identifying azo compounds at 1 mg/L(B), 0.25 mg/L(C), 2 mg/L(D).
Comparison of the constructed sensor with existed methods for detecting SOG.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Diode array detection. bSolid phase extraction. cOne-step air-assisted liquid–liquid microextraction.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of SOG in three water samples.

head.ce:title: Construction of fluorescent sensor array with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensing Sudan Orange G and identification of various azo compounds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were facilely fabricated by one-pot hydrothermal method with levulinic acid and triethanolamine. A fluorescent sensor array was established for identifying azo compounds including Sudan Orange G (SOG), p-diaminoazobenzene, p-aminoazobenzene, azobenzene and quantitative detection of SOG. Experimental results revealed that azo compounds could quench the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs. Owing to various azo compounds showing different affinities to N-CDs, the sensor array exhibited different fluorescence quenching changes, which were further analyzed with principal component analysis to discriminate azo compounds. The sensor array was able to differentiate and recognize diverse concentrations of azo compounds from 0.25 to 2 mg/L. Simultaneously, a variety of factors affecting the detection of SOG were optimized. Under the optimized conditions, the sensor showed excellent stability and sensitivity. The sensor possessed marvelous linearity in the range of 0.1–1 mg/L and 1–4 mg/L and the detection limit was 27.82 μg/L. Spiked recoveries of 90.8–98.2 % were attained at spiked levels of 0.2 mg/L and 1 mg/L, demonstrating that the constructed fluorescence sensor was dependable and feasible for sensing SOG in environmental water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In general, azo compounds are synthetic fat-soluble organic colorants characterized by a chromogenic azo group (-NN-) [1]. Azo compounds are frequently used in a variety of industries including food, textiles and petroleum. In addition, they are also potential contaminants in natural water samples [2], and pose a serious hazard to the health of organisms and ecological system due to the complexity of the water bodies, high lipophilicity and easy bioaccumulation [3]. Azo compounds are mostly irritants and harmful to the human body, irritating the eyes, respiratory system and skin, and also have significant carcinogenic properties on account of the reduction of their metabolites in the body through the cleavage of these groups, resulting in the formation of mutagenic as well as carcinogenic aromatic amines [4]. Hence, it is essential to explore fast, accessible and sensitive analytical techniques for monitoring azo compounds.

Up to date, various methods have been designed and developed for detecting azo compounds consisting of gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [5,6], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [7,8], high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [9,10] and electrochemical detection [11,12], etc. But most of these methods are complicated to operate and have a long detection period. Fluorescence analysis has been focused for its accessible operation, short analysis time and excellent selectivity. Usually, fluorescent reagents are the main core, and traditional fluorescent reagents or organic dyes are difficult to be synthesized. Recently, semiconductor quantum dots emerged and exhibited good fluorescent properties and achieved many applications. However, most of them have been confirmed to be toxic and pose a serious health threat. Carbon-based nanomaterials are a large family of carbon allotropes, such as carbon aerogels, multi-walled carbon nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, carbon dots, etc. Due to their simple and economical synthesis method, easy functionalization, various excellent physical and chemical properties and environmental friendliness, carbon-based nanomaterials have been widely used [13–16]. Carbon-based nanomaterials are not only easier to prepare than organic dyes, but also less toxic than semiconductor quantum dots and have less environmental impact. Carbon dots (CDs), a new carbonaceous nanomaterial emerging like a shining star, possess good fluorescent properties, biocompatibility, environmental friendliness and low cost, have exhibited good merits increasingly in fields such as sensing [17,18], photocatalysis [19,20], bioimaging [21,22], drug delivery [23,24], optoelectronics [25,26] and theranostics [27–29]. In these applications, CDs are typically employed in various sensing owing to excellent optical properties and large number of surface functional groups. In addition, researchers often improve the physical and chemical properties of CDs by heteroatom doping. Nitrogen doping is the most common heteroatom doping method of CDs. Nitrogen element has strong electronegativity. The outermost layer of its electronic structure contains five valence electrons, and also contains lone pairs of electrons that can transmit electrons. These properties enable the nitrogen element to bind to the carbon element in the CDs and change the electronic structure and optical properties of CDs. Nitrogen doping can often cause red shift of CDs and enhance the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs [30,31]. Furthermore, nitrogen-doped CDs can generally provide abundant hydrophilic functional groups such as –NH2 for CDs to improve the water solubility of CDs. In order to obtain CDs with high quantum and product yields, Ren and coworkers fabricated N,S-doped CDs with methyl orange as the only precursor by hydrothermal method and the product yield and quantum yield of the prepared carbon dots reached 75.6 % and 29.4 %. Their established sensitive fluorescent probe showed high selectivity, low detection limit and fast response for the detection of trace mercury in water [32]. Mohanta’s group synthesized green carbon dots with Gandha Prasarini leaves as biomass precursors and constructed sensitive fluorescent probe for detecting tartrazine in food samples. The synthetized N,S-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent green fluorescence emission and good stability with excitation wavelength at 450 nm [33]. It is worth noting that these conventional sensors described above are generally single sensing unit that can only respond with specific target analyte, making it is more difficult to differentiate and identify multiple target analytes. Development of sensor array can be a good solution to the problem.

Sensor array based on cross-response sensing units have been applied to the detection and differentiation of a series of target analytes or different target analytes with similar structural or chemical characteristics. Yu’s group prepared CDs possessing abundant amino groups by microwave-assisted method, and the amino groups could form −CN bonds with aldehyde groups of the isomers of hydroxybenzaldehyde and nitrobenzaldehyde through Schiff base reaction, which led to fluorescence quenching of carbon dots. Based on this mechanism, they constructed a fluorescent sensor array and combined it with principal component analysis (PCA) to discriminate and identify six isomers of hydroxybenzaldehyde and nitrobenzaldehyde, which has been validated to have the ability to distinguish their mixtures [34]. Xu and coworkers constructed a fluorescent sensor array with CDs and a lanthanide complex, which was confirmed to effectively identify and discriminate seven different heavy metal ions in complicated samples [35]. Hence, the development of fluorescent sensor array is an effective way to discriminate multiple target analytes.

Levulinic acid not only shows good stability and hydrophilicity, but also contains carbonyl and carboxyl groups, which can provide abundant oxygen-containing functional groups for the synthesized CDs, and increase the water solubility and dispersibility of the synthesized CDs [36]. Herein, N-CDs with stable fluorescence were synthesized using triethanolamine and levulinic acid as precursors by one-pot hydrothermal method, an efficient fluorescent sensor array with as-synthesized N-CDs was constructed to discriminate four azo compounds with similar structures and quantitatively detecting SOG. The related parameters were examined. PCA was performed according to quenched fluorescent intensity changes of N-CDs with addition of azo compounds. The illustration of the synthesis of N-CDs and principle of sensor array for discriminating azo compounds and quantitatively detecting SOG were exhibited in Fig. 1 .

Levulinic acid, Sudan Orange G (SOG), p-diaminoazobenzene (pDAAB), p-aminoazobenzene (pAAB), azobenzene (AB) were achieved from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The other reagents used and instruments for characterization could be found in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal preparation of nitrogen doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation procedure of nitrogen doped carbon dots was as described: 0.18 g levulinic acid, 15 mL triethanolamine and 30 mL of pure water were mixed and transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave reactor and reacted for 12 h at 180 °C. Further it was cooled to ambient temperature, and brownish yellow solution was gained, which was filtered with a 0.22 μm syringe filter, then the obtained solution was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (MCWO, 1000 Da) for 24 h. The dialyzed solution was freeze-dried in a vacuum freeze dryer to obtain the amber oily liquid. Finally, N-CDs solution was configured with ultra-pure water and kept in refrigerator at 4 °C.

50 μL of 200 g/LN-CDs solution and a specified concentration of SOG solution were mixed in a centrifuge tube, diluted to 1 mL with ultra-pure water, transferred to a quartz cuvette, and measured on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer. Changes of fluorescence intensity were the key for the quantitative analysis of SOG. The analytical processes of pDAAB, pAAB and AB were as the same as that of SOG. In the process, Excitation and emission wavelengths were controlled at 340 nm and 425 nm, respectively. The slit widths were all kept at 5 nm.

To evaluate feasibility of practical application, three waters were collected from Beijing reclaimed water, diequan spring and Guizhou tiexi river. All water samples were filtered with 0.22 μm syringe filters and reserved in refrigerator at 4℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nitrogen doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphology of fabricated N-CDs was analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM). As exhibited in Fig. 2
A, the as-fabricated N-CDs owned near spherical structure and fabulous dispersity, and their particle sizes were ranging from 2.45 nm to 4.25 nm. The lattice spacing of N-CDs was demonstrated to be 0.203 nm. Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs with various excitation wavelengths were depicted in Fig. 2 B and C. The emission wavelength occurred red-shift with the rise of excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs changed with the change of excitation wavelength, indicating that N-CDs owned excitation wavelength dependence. Fluorescence signals of N-CDs increased with the rise of excitation wavelength from 325 to 340 nm, then decreased with the continuous increase of excitation wavelength. Emission fluorescence intensity reached maximum under exciting at 340 nm. Fig. 2 D depicts the FT-IR spectra for characterizing the functional groups of N-CDs. The characteristic peak at 3407 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH [37]. The stretching vibration of CH3 and CH2 were observed at 2957 and 2890 cm−1 [38]. Furthermore, the peaks at 1644 cm−1, 1408 cm−1 and 1030 cm−1 were contributed to CO, CN and CO stretching vibrations [39,40], respectively. These data indicated that N-CDs contained water-soluble hydroxyl groups, and which were conducive to enhance the hydrophilicity and stability of N-CDs [41]. Fig. 2 E shows the XPS analysis of N-CDs. Three obvious characteristic peaks appeared in XPS survey spectrum, indicating that the prepared N-CDs contained three elements of C, N, and O, and the corresponding proportions of these three elements were 68.25 %, 8.15 % and 23.60 %, respectively. The peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.7 eV and 287.6 eV in C1s spectrum were attributed to CC, CN and CO, respectively [42]. While peaks at 398.7 eV, 402.0 eV and 406.0 eV in N1s spectrum were related to CN, graphite N and oxidized N, respectively [43]. The peaks at 531.8 eV and 530.2 eV in O1s spectrum represented CO and CO, respectively [44]. These results further indicated that the surface of N-CDs was rich in functional groups, and N element was successfully doped.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the dosage of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In general, N-CDs have a reabsorption effect at high concentration, so the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs will decrease with the rise of its dosage [45]. Effect of the dosage of N-CDs on quenching effect was examined over range of 2–15 g/L. As shown in Fig. 3
A, As SOG and AB were concerned, the fluorescence quenching value gradually increased with the rise of the dosage of N-CDs, and resulted in peak value at 10 g/L and then gradually decreased due to the reabsorption effect. For pDAAB and pAAB, the fluorescence quenching value rose with the rise of the dosage of N-CDs, and tended to be stable over 10 g/L. Therefore, the dosage of N-CDs was controlled at 10 g/L for subsequent experiments.

Solution pH is very important for the detection and will influence the fluorescent emission of N-CDs. As seen in Fig. S1 A. With the increase of pH, the fluorescent response of N-CDs gradually decreased. Under the acidic condition, protonation of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of N-CDs may cause changes of energy levels, resulting in strong fluorescent response [46]. Under alkaline condition, some of the functional groups may be deprotonated, thus reducing the fluorescent response. In addition, the influence of pH on fluorescence quenching with addition of four azo compounds were depicted in Fig. 3 B, it was observed that fluorescence intensity changes of N-CDs progressively increased when the pH changed from pH 2 to pH 6.5. The maximum value was achieved at pH 6.5 and then it gradually decreased with pH changing from pH 6.5 to pH 10. Since hydrogen ions can react with amino groups on pDAAB and pAAB or hydroxyl groups on SOG in acidic environment, the interaction of N-CDs with these three substances can be reduced, which further reduced fluorescence quenching value. In alkaline environment, electron leaps on specific groups on N-CDs put an important effect on their fluorescent properties. Due to deprotonation of recognition sites, the fluorescent quenching efficiency may be reduced under the alkaline condition [47]. Therefore, pH 6.5 was chosen for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of incubation time on fluorescence quenching of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Incubation time is not only a crucial parameter for achieving stable fluorescent quenching of carbon dots, but also a key factor for establishment of a reliable, fast and sensitive sensor. Too long incubation time will reduce the advantage of the established method for detecting SOG, and shorter incubation time will result in incomplete interaction between N-doped carbon dots and SOG declining the accuracy of the established method. Fig. 3 C depicts that the fluorescence quenching value was almost unchanged from 0 min to 14 min after fortifying SOG to N-CDs solution, which showed that the interaction between N-CDs and SOG was fast and stable. Therefore, the incubation time was controlled at 2 min.

Ionic strength is a crucial parameter which will impact the fluorescent property of N-CDs. It was generally investigated by fortifying NaCl. Fig. S1B demonstrates that fluorescence intensity of N-CDs eventually declined with the addition of NaCl in the range of 0–20 % (w/v), and it might be caused by the ionization of some groups on N-CDs [48]. Fig. 3 D exhibits that the fluorescent quenching value of N-CDs decreased significantly when the concentration of NaCl was rising up from 0 to 1 %, which could be attributed to salting-in effect, where the low concentration of NaCl increased the solubility of SOG in water, which was not conducive to its binding to N-CDs [49]. As the concentration of NaCl continuously increased, fluorescent quenching value increased obviously, and which reached a maximum when the concentration of NaCl increased to 10 %, which might be due to salting-out effect that caused the decreased solubility of SOG in water, thus favoring its binding to N-CDs. With continuous addition of NaCl, fluorescence quenching value decreased. On the one hand, it could be due to the greater viscosity caused by the high concentration of NaCl, which was unfavorable to the binding of SOG to N-CDs. On the other hand, it was probably ascribed to the decrease of fluorescent response of N-CDs in high salinity environment [50].

N-CDs exhibited remarkable quenching with the fortifying SOG, which may be contributed to dynamic quenching effect (DQE), static quenching effect (SQE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [51]. These mechanisms are different in intrinsic formation. In an attempt to further explore the inherent mechanism, UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, SOG and solution of N-CDs + SOG were determined. As illustrated in Fig. 4
A, it can be seen that no new absorption peaks appeared in this sensing system, indicating that there was no formation of nonfluorescent ground-state complex between N-CDs and SOG. This result excluded the possibility of SQE. Fig. 4B illustrates fluorescent emission spectrum of N-CDs and UV–vis spectrum of SOG, and which apparently showed that fluorescent emission spectrum of N-CDs overlapped with UV–vis spectrum of SOG to a large extent, and demonstrated that quenching mechanism was possibly attributed to FRET or IFE. In the cause of distinguishing these two quenching mechanisms, fluorescence lifetimes were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and the solution of N-CDs + SOG were 4.79 and 3.66 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime significantly decreased with addition of SOG. Nevertheless, fluorescence lifetime almost unchanged in IFE [52]. Consequently, the florescence quenching of N-CDs was mainly ascribed to FRET [53,54].

Under the above optimal testing conditions, the relationship between quenching effect of N-CDs and concentration of SOG was explored. SOG achieved a two-stage linear range. As shown in Fig. 5
A and B, a favorable linearity was achieved over concentration ranges of 0.1–1 mg/L and 1–4 mg/L with correlation coefficients of 0.9923 and 0.9914. The limit of detection of SOG was down to 27.82 μg/L. The sensor was not only simple, convenient, easy operation and low cost, but also highly competitive in the detection of SOG in water.

Reliability and stability of the sensor was further verified by intra-day and inter-day precision experiments and stability experiments. In the intra-day precision experiment, fluorescence quenching value of SOG at 1 mg/L was recorded in one day, and the measurement was repeated six times. In the inter-day precision experiment, SOG (1 mg/L) was measured once a day for six days. The results showed that intra-day precision of SOG was 0.76 % and inter-day precision was 1.09 %. Then fluorescence quenching value of N-CDs was determined within 90 days at 1 mg/L of SOG. As shown in Fig. 5 C, F0-F did not change significantly, which indicated that the constructed sensor possessed terrific precision and stability.

At present, most methods for detecting SOG are based on LC-MS or HPLC. Tamim et al. successfully established sensitive determination method of various azo dyes in real samples with LC-MS/MS. The method achieved resulted in a linear range of 1–100 ng mL−1, and the detection limits in the range of 0.7–3.1 μg/L [55]. Chocholouš and co-workers developed an online SPE-UHPLC-UV/Vis method for the detection of five azo dyes. Compared with the traditional HPLC method, this method has comparable high-performance separation and determination, and is easier to operate [57]. The specific comparison results were shown in Table 1 . From the Table, it was obvious that these methods provided good analytical performance, however, most of them required skilled use of operating instruments, cumbersome operation and long response time for detection of SOG. Present method was different from them, and owned the advantages of short response time and simple operation, and good sensitivity for detection of SOG.

Selectivity is a vital factor for the construction of a sensor for detecting SOG. The selectivity of N-CDs for sensing SOG was explored in the following two sets of experiments. Above all, 1 mg L -1 of SOG and 5 mg L -1 of ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ag+) were added to the centrifuge tubes, and then N-CDs were added. Fig. 6
A apparently illustrated that the metal ions nearly had not significant influence on fluorescence response of N-CDs. Next, N-CDs were spiked to the centrifuge tube, then 1 mg/L of SOG and other azo compounds (pDAAB, pAAB, AB, Sudan Ⅰ, Sudan Ⅱ, Sudan Ⅲ and Sudan Ⅳ) were added, respectively. The results were provided in Fig. 6 B. Fig. 6 B demonstrates that the fluorescence quenching value of N-CDs was the most with fortifying SOG among them. The quenched performance of N-CDs varied by fortifying pDAAB, pAAB and AB, which slightly interfered with the detection of SOG. In contrast, the quenched fluorescent change of N-CDs was very similar and small with the presence of each Sudan red dyes (Sudan I − IV). Therefore, in order to further identify SOG, pDAAB, pAAB and AB, the following PCA was carried out for these four substances.

Radar map in Fig. S2A showed a comparison of the responses of four azo compounds to N-CDs at different concentrations. By mapping each value in the data matrix to a color according to a certain pattern, heat map may use color changes to visualize and compare the data. Fig. 7
A illustrates that at the same concentration of target analytes, most intuitive distinction of four azo compounds was made at 10 mg/L as the dosage of N-CDs varied. Moreover, at the same dosage of N-CDs, the higher concentration of target analytes was, the more obvious their identification was. Under the optimal conditions, four azo compounds (1 mg/L) were fortified into a certain concentration of N-CDs, and fluorescent quenching values of N-CDs were measured, which repeated for thrice to acquire a training matrix (1 sensing element × 4 target analytes × 3 replicates) [59]. PCA was used to process matrix in Origin 2023b, which could transform training matrix into few discriminant factors, and engendering a visual PCA scatter plot with two discriminant factors (Factor 1 and Factor 2) as X-axis and Y-axis [60]. As shown in Fig. 7 B, Factor 1 and Factor 2 together explained that the dosage of N-CDs played a dominant role in PCA. The discriminant results indicated that four azo compounds formed four clusters, which were clearly spaced from each other without overlap, and the results of the three repeated experiments were close. Experimental results demonstrated that the sensor array had excellent ability to recognize and discriminate four azo compounds. In addition, the discrimination of target analytes at higher or lower concentrations was further explored, and the results were described in Fig. 7 C and D. It was found that the developed sensor array effectively recognized four azo compounds in the range of 0.25–2 mg/L. It was also found that relative positions of pAAB and AB changed and moved closer to each other at low concentrations, which might be caused by similarity between them. As shown in dendrogram obtained by hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) ( Fig. S2
B and D), various azo compounds could be distinguished. Fig. S2
C shows that pDAAB and pAAB were unable to be well distinguished at 0.25 mg−1. Above results indicated that the sensor array owned fantastic competence to identify and discriminate azo compounds.

For purpose of evaluating the practical application feasibility of N-CDs, three real water samples were sampled from reclaimed water and diequan spring in Beijing, and tiexi river in Guizhou. These samples were treated by filtering with 0.22 μm syringe filters to wipe out impurities and then stored in refrigeration. The blank and spiked water samples at 0.2 mg/L and 1 mg/L SOG were determined by N-CDs. As shown in Table 2 , SOG was undetected in blank water samples. Recoveries of spiked samples ranged from 90.8-98.2 % with relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 2.2 %.

In present work, N-CDs were prepared by a simple and economical one-pot hydrothermal method using levulinic acid and triethanolamine as precursors, and a stable and reliable fluorescent sensor array was established with N-CDs. Fluorescent sensor array achieved different degrees of quenched fluorescent change of N-CDs through the interaction between azo compounds and N-CDs, and further realized discrimination and recognition of four azo compounds by PCA. The sensor provided a low detection limit for the quantitative detection of SOG. The quenching mechanism might be FRET. The spiked experiments confirmed the reliability and applicability of developed sensor, and which indicated that present work provided an excellent reference for development of other sensors based on carbon dots for simultaneously analyzing and identifying multiple pollutants.

Lutong Bu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shuangying Li: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Linchun Nie: Software, Investigation. Liushan Jiang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Guangyu Dong: Investigation, Formal analysis. Denghao Song: Validation, Software, Investigation. Wenjing Liu: Investigation. Xiaodie Geng: Validation, Software, Investigation. Dejing Meng: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Qingxiang Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.04.109.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination and extraction of illegal dyes Sudan orange G and Sudan red 7B using ultrasonic assisted dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction based on deep eutectic solvent followed by derivative UV-VIS spectrophotometry
Int. J. Environ. Anal. Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Azadkish, A. Int. J. Environ. Anal. Chem, 2022, Doi: 10.1080/03067319.2022.2150080

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensor array for the nanomolar detection of azo dyes in water
ACS Sens.

TrAC-Trend. Anal. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of harmful aromatic amine products of azo dyes by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry
